segments.text:
Major Concepts
Cells internalize substances using multiple mechanisms
Phagocytosis is accomplished by the “professional eaters” neutrophils, macrophages and Dendritic cells
Infected cells can break down proteins into peptides, load them on MHC I molecules and then are “presented” to cytotoxic T-cells
Proteins are broken down into peptides then loaded on MHC II molecules and “presented” to helper T-cells
Ligand:           C3b,C4b,C1q     factor H            iC3b              C3b, C3c       IgG            C1q

Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC).
A complex of genes encoding cell surface molecules that are required for antigen presentation to T cells and for rapid graft rejections.  It is called the H-2 complex in the mouse and the HLA complex in humans.

Examples of anchor residues (blue) in nonameric peptides eluted from two class I MHC molecules
Presentation of antigen by Class I MHC involves proteolytic digestion by proteosomes

Summary of Class I MHC antigen presentation
RER
8-10amino acids from intracellular environment

Cytotoxic T
Cell
Protein encoded by MHC I

Class II MHC presentation is accomplished by internalizing antigen, then presentation
Internalization by phagocytosis, receptor mediated endocytosis, endocytosis

In contrast to antibodies or B-cell receptors, which can recognize an antigen alone, T-cell receptors only recognize antigen that has been processed and presented in the context of molecules encoded by the major histocompatibility complex (MHC).

Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC).
A complex of genes encoding cell surface molecules that are required for antigen presentation to T cells and for rapid graft rejections.  It is called the H-2 complex in the mouse and the HLA complex in humans.
